does: This paper introduces Membrane, a self-evolving guardrail system that maintains a Contrastive Safety Memory (CSM) to defend large language models against jailbreaks. The core contribution is a memory structure where each cell pairs conditions for blocking harmful queries with conditions for permitting similar benign requests, indexed by attack strategy. Rather than retraining, Membrane evolves by distilling harmful interactions and benign counterparts into contrastive cells that generalize across topical variants of the same attack mechanism. At inference, retrieved cells ground safety decisions. The method is evaluated on HarmBench and AgentHarm benchmarks, achieving the highest F1 scores across six jailbreak attacks while maintaining low benign refusal rates of 7-14%, substantially lower than prior defense approaches ranging from 28-85%.
connects: This work builds on the established problem of safety alignment in LLMs and addresses known limitations of existing approaches. It extends prior work on memory-based guardrails, which struggle with over-refusing benign queries, and static safety classifiers that cannot adapt to evolving attacks. The paper combines insights from contrastive learning methods with safety memory systems, drawing on the principle that effective safety decisions require distinguishing harmful intent from benign content with similar surface features. It responds to demonstrated vulnerabilities in fine-tuned classifiers against adaptive adversaries and addresses the practical gap between model-level and agent-level safety, where tool-use contexts present unique jailbreak opportunities not fully captured by existing benchmarks.
enables: Membrane points toward continually adaptive safety mechanisms that can keep pace with evolving adversarial strategies without requiring model retraining. The cell-based architecture suggests future directions for interpretable safety decisions, where specific attack patterns and their benign analogs can be inspected and refined. This work enables practical deployment of LLM agents in high-stakes domains by reducing false positive safety rejections while maintaining robustness. The approach also opens research into automatic cell generation and merging, discovering new attack strategies through memory organization, and extending contrastive safety memory to multi-modal or multi-lingual settings where attack strategies vary by domain or language.
